CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear structure (Introduction, Related Work, Preliminaries, Method, Experiments, Conclusion) and the main contribution (an off-policy batch policy-gradient method, BPG) is stated early and revisited throughout (Sec.1 and Sec.3). Notation is generally consistent and well defined (MDP formulation, q(s), q(a|s), objective (1), Algorithms 1–2) which makes the derivations in Sec.3 easy to follow. Figures/tables and concrete examples are referenced and discussed (Fig.1/2, Table 1, Appendix examples), though the actual figures/tables are not shown here; the Appendices give useful implementation details. Presentation has minor issues: several duplicated/garbled sentence blocks and citation repetitions in the addendum harm readability and there are occasional long sentences and formatting artifacts that should be cleaned up.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated contribution: an off-policy, batch policy-gradient algorithm (BPG) tailored to Seq2Seq conversation models with noisy/expensive labels, explicitly using importance sampling, λ-returns computed from offline episodes, and GTD(λ)-based value estimation (Sections 3 and Appendix A). The authors clearly contrast this with prior on-policy/on-line RL work for Seq2Seq (Ranzato et al., Bahdanau et al., Li et al., Williams & Zweig) and leverage batch access to future actions/rewards and unlabelled data to learn behaviour policies (Introduction, Sec. 3–4). While the method composes known RL ingredients (Degris et al., Maei) rather than proposing a fundamentally new learning paradigm, its application and practical adaptations to this setting represent a meaningful, non-trivial extension that would be of interest to experts.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The core derivations are technically sound: the approximation ∇J(θ) ≈ E_{s~q}∑_a ∇πθ(a|s)Qπθ(s,a), the change of measure to data distribution via importance weights ρ=π/q, subtraction of a V baseline, and the λ-return with trajectory importance ratios are standard and correctly applied (Sec.3 eqs.2–3). The use of GTD(λ) for value estimation and practical remedies (clipping ρ, small step sizes, fixing lower LSTM layers) are reasonable implementation choices; the batch adaptation of GTD is presented consistently (App. A). Experimental methodology is mostly sound (synthetic and real AMT labels, paired tests and a nonparametric Wilcoxon), but results show only modest/significant-at-10%-level gains in the real task and several heuristic choices (ρ clipping, fixing part of the network, small labelled set) introduce bias/variance tradeoffs and limit generality. Overall the technical claims are supported with minor practical caveats that do not undermine the main conclusions.
